md: A list of numbers tells you everything but reveals nothing. **Visualizing** data — turning it into shapes you can see — is half the job in statistics. Each display answers a different question.
## 1. Dot Plot
A **dot plot** stacks one dot above each value. Best for **small datasets** where you want to see every point.
**Example.** Test scores: $7, 8, 8, 9, 9, 9, 10$.
You see the count (height) and the values (position) at a glance. Good for categorical or small numeric data.
## 2. Histogram
A **histogram** groups data into **bins** (intervals) and shows the count in each bin as a bar. Best for **larger datasets** where individual points blur together.
**Bins** must be equal-width, adjacent (no gaps), and cover the full range. Choosing the bin width is part art, part science.
**Read off:** approximate range (leftmost to rightmost bin), peaks (modes), shape (symmetric? skewed?), gaps and outliers.

blocks.title: Histogram vs bar chart
blocks.body: **Histogram:** numerical data, bins are intervals, bars touch (no gaps). **Bar chart:** categorical data, bars have gaps. Conceptually different — don't mix them up.

md: Try the histogram below. Switch the dataset (symmetric test scores, bell-shaped heights, right-skewed waiting times, bimodal mixed ages) and slide the **bins** slider from 3 up to 20 — watch the shape go from over-smoothed to noisy to just-right. That's the bin-width trade-off.
## 3. Box Plot (Box-and-Whisker)
A **box plot** displays the **five-number summary** (min, Q1, median, Q3, max) at a glance.
**Box:** stretches from Q1 to Q3 (the middle 50% of data — the IQR is the box width).
**Line in the box:** the median.
**Whiskers:** extend to the min and max (or to a cutoff like $1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ from the box, with outliers plotted separately).
**Excellent for comparing distributions side by side** — overlay multiple box plots and you can see differences in center, spread, and skewness.
## 4. Stem-and-Leaf Plot
A **stem-and-leaf plot** is a compact form between a list and a histogram. The 'stem' is the leading digit(s); the 'leaf' is the trailing digit.
Reads quickly, preserves every original value, and shows the shape like a sideways histogram.
## 5. Shape of a Distribution
Words to describe distribution shape:
**Symmetric:** mirror image around center (e.g., the bell curve).
**Skewed right (positive skew):** long tail on the right. Mean > median.
**Skewed left (negative skew):** long tail on the left. Mean < median.
**Uniform:** roughly flat — every value about as likely.
**Bimodal:** two peaks — often signals two underlying groups mixed together.

blocks.title: Mean vs median tells you the skew
blocks.body: **Mean > median** → likely right-skewed. **Mean < median** → likely left-skewed. **Mean ≈ median** → roughly symmetric. Income distributions are classic right-skew (a few earn vastly more); life expectancies tend to be left-skew (a few die very young).

md: ### Worked example — read a box plot
A box plot for class quiz scores: min $= 60$, Q1 $= 72$, median $= 80$, Q3 $= 88$, max $= 100$.
**Middle 50% of students scored between $72$ and $88$.** Median score $= 80$.
Whiskers are roughly equal length (Q1−min ≈ 12, max−Q3 ≈ 12) → **roughly symmetric**.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Dot plot:** small datasets, see every point. **Histogram:** larger datasets, see the shape. **Box plot:** five-number summary, great for comparing distributions. **Stem-and-leaf:** between a list and a histogram. Shape (symmetric/skewed) and the mean-vs-median comparison tell you a lot at a glance.
